summary: Gruber eviscerates Pentagram's rebranding work for Slack, dissecting the design firm's 'range of possibilities' that accompanied the new logo announcement. He argues that several of the proposed directions reveal a fundamental ignorance of what Slack actually is — from using an '@' symbol (email's identifier) for a product meant to replace email, to incorporating Apple's emoji art as a logo concept. He accuses Pentagram of employing the classic designer's trick of presenting throwaway garbage alongside one real proposal to make the client feel smart for choosing the intended option. He contrasts Pentagram's work with designer Louie Mantia's 10-minute sketch that better addressed Slack's stated problems with their old mark.
key_points: Several of Pentagram's proposed Slack identities betray a fundamental misunderstanding of the product — using '@' for a product that replaces email, and chat bubbles for a product that doesn't use them
Pentagram's 28 monochrome 'octothorpe explorations' mostly don't resemble a hash mark at all, with perhaps one or two exceptions out of 28
The proposals appear to be deliberate throwaway garbage designed to make the real proposal look good by comparison — a classic design industry manipulation tactic
Louie Mantia's 10-minute sketch better addressed Slack's stated branding problems than anything Pentagram formally proposed
The final new Slack mark is dismissed as 'an unmemorable, unpleasant shape' that disregards users' genuine affection for the existing brand
money_quotes: What they're showing is shockingly bad work. I can't believe Pentagram would present some of this to a client, and it's even harder to believe they would show it publicly.
It makes no sense and looks like someone spent 30 seconds making it in TextEdit. It looks more like a tweet than a logo.
Whoever crapped this logo out clearly didn't even know what Slack is.
This one looks like a man bouncing a ball behind a barber chair.
If I were on Slack's marketing team and Pentagram showed me these proposals, I'd look around the room for hidden cameras, presuming that I was being pranked.

This is the oldest trick in the designer's book — making one real proposal and then a bunch of throwaway garbage proposals to create the illusion of multiple directions for the client to choose from, and assuming the rube client will happily accept the real one and consider themselves smart for knowing which one was the best.
Their new mark is nothing more than an unmemorable, unpleasant shape.
key_insight: Pentagram's Slack rebrand reveals either a cynical manipulation tactic of presenting garbage alternatives to steer clients, or a fundamental failure to understand the product they were hired to represent.
